summary: Gruber examines how Trump's sweeping tariffs, particularly the 54% levy on Chinese imports, clash with Apple's deeply ingrained practice of keeping retail prices stable across product generations. He illustrates Apple's pricing philosophy with examples like the trashcan Mac Pro maintaining its $2,999 price for years without updates, and the current M2 Ultra Mac Pro still at $6,999 even as newer M3 Ultra Mac Studios exist at $3,999. Apple's stock dropped 16.6% in 48 hours as markets reacted to the tariff announcements. Gruber predicts Apple will absorb the tariff costs rather than raise prices, but notes this would mean breaking even or losing money on every iPhone sold. The fundamental tension is between Apple's brand-defining price stability and the unpredictable, nonsensical nature of the tariffs.
key_points: Apple treats retail pricing as a core part of its brand identity, almost never changing prices between product generations regardless of market conditions
Trump's 54% tariff on Chinese imports could theoretically push iPhone prices up 43%, making a base iPhone 16 cost $1,142 and a maxed-out Pro Max nearly $2,300
Apple lost 16.6% of its market value in just 48 hours following the tariff announcement
The tariffs' unpredictability is especially damaging to a company that has maintained pricing stability for decades
Apple will likely absorb tariff costs to maintain price stability, but would be breaking even or losing money on each iPhone sold
money_quotes: Apple, to a point of almost obstinance, considers pricing part of the brand for its products.
They tend not to raise or lower prices with the ebbs and flows of the world economy or even the obvious constraints of simple supply and demand.
That's how Apple likes to do pricing: they set a price when a product is announced, and that price never changes until a successor to that product is announced.
The erratic, illogical, nonsensical nature of Trump's tariffs is bad for everyone.
It's particularly troublesome for a company that sees retail price stability as part of the branding for its products.
In addition to being nonsensical, the whole thing is entirely unpredictable, which is not at all compatible with the way Apple has set retail prices for decades.
My (bigly) guess is that Apple will inject its own predictability and stability into the mix, and keep its retail prices stable, for now, and take the tariff hit on its margins.
key_insight: Apple's near-religious commitment to price stability across product generations creates a uniquely painful collision with unpredictable tariffs, forcing them to choose between brand identity and profitability.
